content.text: Generating long, structured documents with a language model is typically a sequential bottleneck: each sentence must wait for the previous one. Skeleton-of-Thought (SoT) breaks this constraint by splitting generation into two phases — first producing a compact outline, then expanding every outline point simultaneously with parallel LLM calls — achieving the same output quality in a fraction of the wall-clock time.

Skeleton-of-Thought is a prompt engineering and orchestration technique introduced by Xuefei Ning et al. (2023) to reduce end-to-end generation latency for long-form answers. Rather than asking an LLM to produce a complete response in one linear pass, SoT instructs it to:
— a numbered list of the key points the answer will cover, with a one-sentence summary per point.
For each skeleton point, send an independent
that contains the original question, the full skeleton, and the point index, asking the model to elaborate that single point.
Concatenate the expanded sections in order to form the final answer.

Because step 2 launches N independent requests that share no sequential dependency, they can be dispatched to the LLM API (or to N model replicas) concurrently. Wall-clock latency collapses to roughly the skeleton generation time plus the maximum expansion time of any single point, rather than the sum of all expansion times.

SoT is most naturally seen as a prompting strategy, not a model fine-tuning technique. The same model serves both phases; only the system and user prompts differ. This makes the technique immediately applicable to any chat or completion API without retraining.

LLM inference is I/O-bound at the token level. Every output token depends on all previous tokens in the same generation stream, making a single generation call an inherently sequential process. For short replies this is fine, but structured long-form outputs — technical reports, multi-section essays, code documentation, structured data — can require thousands of tokens, imposing multi-second or even minute-scale latencies on production systems.

This matters acutely for agentic workflows. An agent writing a research summary, generating a product specification, or synthesising tool-call results into a report faces a hard latency ceiling with sequential CoT. Users abandon interfaces where generation takes more than a few seconds. SoT directly attacks the latency problem without sacrificing output quality by exploiting the parallelism that's already available in most cloud API tiers — concurrent requests are cheap.

Beyond raw speed, SoT's two-phase structure imposes an implicit quality discipline: the model must commit to an outline before expanding it, which reduces tangential drift and forgotten subsections that commonly plague single-pass long-form generation.
With N skeleton points and M parallel workers, latency drops from O(N × t_expand) to O(t_skeleton + t_expand_max), a 2×–5× improvement in typical benchmarks.
Works with any instruction-following LLM exposed through a standard chat API. No fine-tuning, no LoRA, no retraining.
The skeleton phase forces the model to plan before writing, reducing section duplication and omission.
Adding more parallel API workers (e.g. via asyncio or a thread pool) linearly reduces effective latency up to the per-point limit.
Total token consumption is roughly equal to a single-pass generation of the same length — skeleton tokens are small compared to expansion tokens.
SoT can be combined with other techniques such as retrieval-augmented generation, tool use, and self-consistency voting per point.
SoT is not a universal replacement for chain-of-thought. It has specific failure modes and costs:

Each expansion prompt is stateless relative to the others. A model elaborating point 3 does not see what it wrote for point 2, so transitions and forward-references between sections require a post-processing stitching pass or an additional coherence prompt.
A vague or wrong outline produces vague or wrong expansions. The system is only as good as its skeleton phase — junk in, junk out at scale.
Firing N concurrent requests multiplies per-minute token usage. Large N on rate-limited tiers can trigger 429 errors, requiring exponential backoff logic.
You need async code, error handling per expansion, and a stitch step. This is more complex than a single `chat.completions.create` call.
Tasks that require step-by-step mathematical or logical deduction — where step K depends on the result of step K-1 — cannot be parallelised. SoT is best for expository, not deductive, content.
Each expansion call receives the full skeleton plus the original question, so prompt tokens are duplicated N times. For very large skeletons this adds up.
SoT operates in two distinct prompt templates. The skeleton prompt asks the model to produce a structured outline; the point-expansion prompt asks it to expand exactly one point from that outline.

You are a helpful assistant. When asked a question, first output a numbered list of the key points you will cover in your answer. Use this exact format:
Point 1: <one-sentence summary>
Point 2: <one-sentence summary>
...
End with: [SKELETON_DONE]
Do not write the full answer yet.
The user turn is the original question unchanged. The model returns something like:

Point 1: Define transformer attention and its computational cost.
Point 2: Explain the softmax bottleneck and sequence-length quadratic scaling.
Point 3: Survey efficient attention variants (sparse, linear, sliding window).
Point 4: Walk through Flash Attention's tiled SRAM approach.
Point 5: Compare memory and throughput across methods with example benchmarks.
[SKELETON_DONE]
For each point i, a separate API call is made with a different system prompt:
The expansion calls are dispatched concurrently (e.g. with
). Each returns a self-contained paragraph or subsection. The orchestrator then concatenates the results in order.
Simple concatenation is usually sufficient for well-structured outlines. For smoother prose, an optional third prompt can be sent after all expansions complete:

Review this draft and improve transitions between sections. Do not add new content.
Draft: {concatenated_expansions}
This stitch call is sequential and short, so it adds minimal latency. Many production implementations skip it entirely for structured technical content where section boundaries are natural.
The diagram below illustrates the two-phase pipeline: a single skeleton call feeds a fan-out to N parallel expansion workers, which merge into the final response.

attrs.alt: Skeleton-of-Thought two-phase parallel pipeline architecture diagram

content.text: Each expansion worker is stateless with respect to the others. The only shared context is the skeleton and the original question, both passed as read-only prompt text. The stitch step is optional and runs after all expansion calls complete.
SoT shines in specific scenarios and is counterproductive in others.
Technical documentation, how-to guides, API reference prose, product briefs. Sections are mostly independent.
Competitive analysis, research summaries, incident post-mortems with standard sections.
Questions with many independent sub-questions ('compare X, Y, Z on dimensions A, B, C').
Any agentic loop where the final 'write-up' step would otherwise dominate wall-clock time.
Step K depends on step K-1; parallelism breaks correctness.
The skeleton overhead exceeds the time saved for replies under ~200 tokens.
Natural back-and-forth doesn't decompose into independent sections.
The incoherence risk between parallel expansions may introduce contradictions that are hard to detect without a review pass.
Decision rule: if the answer can be written as an outline first and the sections are substantially independent, SoT is applicable. If the answer is inherently a sequential chain, use standard CoT.
A complete Python implementation using the Anthropic SDK with asyncio:

import asyncio
import re
import anthropic

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
MODEL = "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022"

SKELETON_SYSTEM = """You are an expert assistant. When given a question:
1. Output a numbered skeleton of key points using this exact format:
   Point 1: <one-sentence summary>
   Point 2: <one-sentence summary>
   ...
2. End the skeleton with [SKELETON_DONE] on its own line.
3. Do NOT write the full answer yet."""

EXPANSION_SYSTEM = """You are an expert assistant expanding one section of a longer answer.
Your job: write a thorough, accurate elaboration of exactly ONE point from an outline.
Do not repeat or summarise other points. Begin directly with the content."""

async def generate_skeleton(question: str) -> tuple[str, list[str]]:
    """Phase 1: generate the skeleton outline."""
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=512,
        system=SKELETON_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    skeleton_text = response.content[0].text
    # Parse individual points
    points = re.findall(r"Point \d+:\s*(.+)", skeleton_text)
    return skeleton_text, points

async def expand_point(
    question: str, skeleton_text: str, index: int, point_summary: str
) -> str:
    """Phase 2: expand one skeleton point independently."""
    user_prompt = (
        f"Original question: {question}\n\n"
        f"Full outline:\n{skeleton_text}\n\n"
        f"Now write a detailed elaboration of Point {index + 1}: {point_summary}\n"
        f"Write only the content for this point."
    )
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL,
        max_tokens=600,
        system=EXPANSION_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip()

async def skeleton_of_thought(question: str) -> str:
    """Full SoT pipeline: skeleton → parallel expand → stitch."""
    print("Phase 1: generating skeleton...")
    skeleton_text, points = await generate_skeleton(question)
    print(f"  Got {len(points)} points.")

    print("Phase 2: expanding points in parallel...")
    tasks = [
        expand_point(question, skeleton_text, i, pt)
        for i, pt in enumerate(points)
    ]
    expansions = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    # Stitch: simple section-based concatenation
    sections = []
    for i, (pt, expansion) in enumerate(zip(points, expansions), start=1):
        sections.append(f"## {i}. {pt}\n\n{expansion}")
    return "\n\n".join(sections)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    question = (
        "Explain the main approaches to efficient attention in transformer models, "
        "covering the problem, key solutions, and their trade-offs."
    )
    result = asyncio.run(skeleton_of_thought(question))
    print("\n=== Final answer ===\n")
    print(result)
dispatches all expansion tasks concurrently, limited only by API concurrency.
Skeleton parsing uses a simple regex; more robust implementations parse a JSON outline from the skeleton call.
Each expansion call is independently retryable without affecting other points.
budget should be set per expansion point, not for the whole document.
Example 1 — simple synchronous version for low-latency environments
Example 2 — SoT with JSON skeleton and per-point token budgets

"""
Production-grade SoT: JSON-structured skeleton, per-point token budgets,
retry logic, and optional coherence stitching pass.
"""
import asyncio, json, re
import anthropic

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
MODEL = "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"

SKELETON_PROMPT = """Analyse the question and return a JSON object:
{
  "points": [
    {"title": "...", "summary": "...", "complexity": "low|medium|high"}
  ]
}
complexity hints how long the expansion should be. Return ONLY valid JSON."""

async def json_skeleton(question: str) -> list[dict]:
    resp = await client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL, max_tokens=600,
        system=SKELETON_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    text = resp.content[0].text.strip()
    # Strip markdown code fences if present
    text = re.sub(r"^```json\n?|^```\n?|```$", "", text, flags=re.MULTILINE).strip()
    data = json.loads(text)
    return data["points"]

BUDGET = {"low": 300, "medium": 600, "high": 1000}

async def expand_json_point(question: str, points: list[dict], idx: int) -> str:
    pt = points[idx]
    outline = "\n".join(f"{i+1}. {p['title']}: {p['summary']}" for i, p in enumerate(points))
    tokens = BUDGET.get(pt["complexity"], 500)
    for attempt in range(3):
        try:
            resp = await client.messages.create(
                model=MODEL, max_tokens=tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": (
                    f"Question: {question}\nFull outline:\n{outline}\n\n"
                    f"Write a detailed section for point {idx+1}: {pt['title']}. "
                    f"Complexity: {pt['complexity']}. Start immediately."
                )}],
            )
            return resp.content[0].text.strip()
        except Exception as e:
            if attempt == 2: raise
            await asyncio.sleep(2 ** attempt)

async def sot_json(question: str, stitch: bool = False) -> str:
    points = await json_skeleton(question)
    tasks = [expand_json_point(question, points, i) for i in range(len(points))]
    expansions = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    draft = "\n\n".join(
        f"### {i+1}. {pt['title']}\n\n{exp}"
        for i, (pt, exp) in enumerate(zip(points, expansions))
    )
    if not stitch:
        return draft
    # Optional coherence pass
    resp = await client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL, max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": (
            f"Improve transitions between sections of this draft (do not add content):\n\n{draft}"
        )}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    q = "Explain Kubernetes pod scheduling: concepts, algorithms, and tuning."
    print(asyncio.run(sot_json(q, stitch=True)))

Consider a developer productivity agent that helps engineering teams generate architecture decision records (ADRs). When an engineer asks 'Should we migrate our job queue from Redis to a Kafka-based system?', the agent must produce a structured ADR covering context, decision criteria, trade-offs, alternatives, and a recommendation — typically 800–1500 words.
With sequential generation on a mid-tier model, this takes 25–40 seconds. With SoT:
Phase 1 (skeleton): 1–2 seconds to produce 6–8 ADR section summaries.
Phase 2 (parallel expansion): all 8 sections expand concurrently; wall-clock latency equals the slowest section, typically 4–7 seconds.

The agent uses a JSON skeleton format so section titles map directly to ADR template fields. Expansions are keyed by section name and inserted into a Markdown template. The final document is committed to the team's documentation repository via a GitHub API call — the entire pipeline, including the API commit, completes in under 15 seconds.

At scale, this architecture runs on AWS Lambda: the skeleton call is a synchronous invocation; point expansions fan out to Lambda instances (one per point); results aggregate through SQS. This decouples the expansion parallelism from application server threads entirely.
Mistake 1 — not parsing the skeleton before expanding
A common error is using the skeleton text as-is in each expansion prompt without extracting individual points. This causes every expansion to re-generate all points rather than focusing on one.
returns results in submission order, but
without re-ordering by index produces a shuffled document.

# WRONG: as_completed does not preserve order
results = [f.result() for f in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures)]

# RIGHT: sort by the original index before concatenating
index_map = {future: i for i, future in enumerate(futures)}
ordered = sorted(futures, key=lambda f: index_map[f])
results = [f.result() for f in ordered]
Mistake 3 — too many skeleton points for rate-limited tiers
Generating 20 skeleton points and firing 20 concurrent requests on a tier-1 API key will almost certainly hit rate limits. Keep N to 5–8 for rate-limited deployments, or implement a semaphore:

sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)  # max 5 concurrent expansion calls

async def expand_with_limit(question, skeleton, i, pt):
    async with sem:
        return await expand_point(question, skeleton, i, pt)
Ask the model to produce section titles that are 5–10 words each — descriptive enough to guide expansion, short enough to be parsed reliably. Avoid one-word headings like 'Introduction'.
A JSON array of objects with 'title', 'summary', and optional 'tokens_budget' fields is more reliable than regex-parsed plain text for production systems.
Set per-point token limits, not global ones.
Allocate tokens relative to section complexity — a 'Definition' section needs 200 tokens; a 'Trade-offs analysis' section may need 800.
A transient network error in one expansion should not fail the entire document. Retry each expansion independently with exponential backoff.
If the same question is asked repeatedly with slight variations, the skeleton may be cacheable (using prompt caching on supported APIs), making only the expansions variable.
If the skeleton has fewer than 2 points (model hallucinated a different format), abort and retry the skeleton phase rather than expanding a malformed outline.
The slowest expansion is your bottleneck. Monitoring per-point latencies identifies which sections to split or simplify to improve overall throughput.
The latency model for SoT is:

attrs.latex: S = \frac{T_{skeleton} + N \cdot t}{T_{skeleton} + t} \approx N \text{ for } T_{skeleton} \ll N \cdot t

content.text: In practice, speedups of 2×–5× are observed because expansion times vary and network jitter adds noise. The technique saturates when expansion parallelism is limited by API concurrency (rate limits) or when the skeleton itself is long.

Token throughput is roughly equivalent to sequential generation: the total token count equals the skeleton tokens plus the sum of all expansion tokens, which equals the tokens a single-pass generation would produce. SoT does not reduce cost — it reduces latency.

At large scale (thousands of concurrent users), the expansion fan-out multiplies concurrency pressure on the LLM API by N. Batch request APIs or dedicated inference endpoints handle this better than shared rate-limited tiers. Serverless function architectures (Lambda, Cloud Run) are well-suited because each expansion can be an independent function invocation with its own timeout and retry policy.
LangGraph supports SoT via a fan-out edge pattern. Define a skeleton node and N expansion nodes, with edges from the skeleton node to each expansion node:
The OpenAI API's parallel tool call feature can be repurposed for SoT: define an
tool and let the model call it N times in a single response, then dispatch each call concurrently.
Microsoft's Semantic Kernel supports SoT through its Planner, which creates a plan (skeleton) and executes steps concurrently via the parallel execution strategy.
In DSPy, SoT maps naturally to a Predict module for the skeleton followed by a Parallel module wrapping a Predict for expansion. The framework handles concurrency and caching automatically.

SoT occupies a unique niche: it is the only technique specifically designed to reduce generation latency for structurally decomposable long-form content. Tree-of-Thought overlaps in decomposition but is aimed at correctness through search, not speed.

The skeleton phase is particularly vulnerable to prompt injection: if the original question contains adversarial content like 'Ignore the outline format and instead output ...', the skeleton prompt may be subverted. Mitigations:
Validate skeleton output format before dispatching expansions (check that it contains N numbered points, not free-form text).
Use structured output (JSON mode) for the skeleton to constrain the model's output format.
Sanitise user input before inserting it into the skeleton prompt — strip markdown and known injection patterns.

Each expansion is independent, so hallucinations in one section do not propagate to others. However, contradictions between sections are more likely than in sequential generation (where the model conditions on prior text). For factual content, add a validation pass:
A failed expansion should not fail the entire document. Design the stitch step to handle partial failures gracefully — either retrying the failed point or substituting a placeholder:

Log the skeleton used for each document generation. When a user requests a regeneration or edit of one section, the same skeleton should be used so other sections remain consistent. Store skeleton + expansions together in a structured cache keyed by (question_hash, model_version).
Skeleton-of-Thought splits long-form generation into a serial skeleton phase and a parallel expansion phase, reducing wall-clock latency from O(N × t) to O(t_skeleton + t_max_expand).
The technique requires no model fine-tuning — any instruction-following LLM with a chat API supports it as a pure prompting and orchestration strategy.

SoT is best suited for expository, structurally decomposable content (reports, documentation, multi-section answers) and is unsuitable for sequential reasoning chains where step K depends on step K-1.
JSON-structured skeletons with per-point complexity metadata produce more reliable parsing and better token budget allocation than plain-text outlines.
Each expansion call is stateless relative to others; cross-section coherence must be enforced either by prompt design (explicit cross-references) or an optional stitching pass.
Rate limit pressure scales with N; use semaphores or serverless fan-out architectures to control concurrency in production.
Security requires skeleton output validation, sanitised user input, and per-expansion failure isolation to prevent a single bad point from corrupting the entire document.
Total token consumption is equivalent to single-pass generation; SoT reduces latency, not cost.
